review_text: Not such a great way to fly. I booked an air ticket and the flight departure timing was brought forward by 7 hours. We had the option to accept, reschedule or cancel to get a refund. I chose the third option but was put thru multiple individuals who do not seem to know what they are doing (each contradicting the other) but eventually, I received an email to say the refund was being processed and it would take 6 weeks. Well, it was the 6th week yesterday and I wrote in to enquire, only to be told they will be processing it now. Today I received another email (a proper refund notice this time) to say it will be another 6 weeks for the refund to come thru.
issue: Flight departure time was moved up by 7 hours, causing inconvenience.
fixes: Deploy an automated schedule‑change notification workflow in your CRM (e.g., Salesforce Service Cloud) that triggers a personalized email/SMS within 15 minutes of any flight‑time adjustment, including the new departure date/time and a “rebook now” link to available alternative flights. | - Integrate a real‑time disruption monitoring dashboard with FlightAware APIs into the airline’s operations center so dispatchers can instantly see affected itineraries and automatically reassign seats on other flights when a 7‑hour shift occurs. | - Launch a self‑service rebooking portal built on Amadeus Altéa, allowing passengers to modify bookings or request compensation without contacting call centers, with a 24‑hour SLA for processing requests. | - Implement a rule‑based compensation engine in Sabre Red Workspace that automatically issues travel vouchers or mileage credits for flights whose departure times are changed by more than 6 hours, ensuring consistent treatment across all routes. | - Establish a dedicated “Schedule Change Response Team” staffed by two agents per shift, tracked via a Zendesk ticket queue with a target first‑response time of ≤5 minutes, and log each case in a Power BI KPI report to monitor adherence weekly.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant, actionable, and concrete, naming specific tools (Salesforce, FlightAware, Amadeus Altéa, Sabre Red Workspace, Zendesk, Power BI). However, feasibility is scored 2 due to high effort: deploying a dedicated team, integrating multiple third-party APIs, building a self-service portal, and implementing rule-based engines across systems requires significant budget, cross-team coordination, and months of development—far beyond trivial or low-effort implementation.

issue: Customer service representatives gave contradictory information and seemed incompetent.
fixes: Deploy a single‑source knowledge hub (e.g., Zendesk Guide) and require all agents to complete a mandatory “Knowledge Sync” checklist before their shift, verifying they’ve reviewed the latest policy articles within the past 24 hours. | - Institute a weekly “Shadow Review” where senior specialists record agent calls in Gong.io, score them against a competency rubric, and provide instant feedback via Slack bots; set a minimum pass threshold (≥90 % on the rubric) for continued call routing. | - Launch a real‑time conflict‑resolution dashboard in Power BI that pulls data from the ticketing system to flag any duplicate answers or contradictory advice, triggering automatic alerts to supervisors for immediate coaching. | - Implement a role‑play certification program in Lessonly, requiring agents to complete at least two scenario simulations per month, with performance scores tied to quarterly bonus eligibility and published on an internal leaderboard.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the root issue of contradictory information and incompetence. Each is specific, actionable, and names real tools (Zendesk Guide, Gong.io, Power BI, Lessonly) with clear metrics. Feasibility is penalized to 3 because implementing a role-play certification tied to bonuses and a real-time dashboard requires cross-team coordination, new workflows, and likely budget for software integration and training—moderate effort, not trivial.

issue: Refund processing took excessively long, with repeated 6‑week delays.
fixes: Deploy a refund workflow in Salesforce Service Cloud that auto‑escalates any case older than 7 days to a senior specialist queue and logs SLA breaches for real‑time KPI tracking. | - Integrate Stripe’s Refund API with your ERP (e.g., SAP) so refunds are posted instantly and trigger automated email notifications to customers via SendGrid within 24 hours of approval. | - Set up a weekly “Refund Audit” Power BI report that flags cases >10 business days pending, assigns them a priority flag, and routes them to a dedicated rapid‑resolution team. | - Implement a RACI matrix in Asana for all refund tasks, assigning owners, approvers, and due dates; enforce a mandatory 48‑hour review cycle before any refund is marked complete.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the 6-week refund delay issue with specific tools (Salesforce, Stripe, SAP, SendGrid, Power BI, Asana) and measurable actions. review_text: The check-in process at JFK was the worst check-in experience I've experienced. I was waiting for check-in for over 2 hours. The line just didn't move at all. The agent was extremely inefficient. I was one of the last customers to check in and it was about 30 minutes before the departure time. The counter agents was escorting a group of last-to-check-in customers to the security checkpoint so they could skip the line (we were late) and somehow I was the only one who left in the security line on my own. The service on board was terrible despite the high-regarded in-flight service that Singapore Airlines is usually known for. The service was so slow for a flight with only 160 customers.  I sat at one of the single seat toward the end of the aircraft and the steward kept bumping into me like I was sitting on the aisle. He didn't even apologize. My food was OK at best. The worst part was probably the lavatories. Truly disgusting, trash was everywhere on the floor.
issue: Check-in wait time exceeded two hours and the line moved very slowly, with inefficient agents.
fixes: Deploy a cloud‑based queue management platform (e.g., QLess) integrated with the property’s POS to display real‑time wait estimates on digital signage and push SMS alerts, reducing perceived wait time by at least 15 %. | - Implement a workforce‑management solution such as Kronos Workforce Central to forecast peak check‑in windows based on historical arrival patterns and automatically schedule additional agents 30 minutes before predicted spikes. | - Launch a mobile pre‑check‑in app that captures guest ID and payment details via QR code scanning, then route those records directly into the PMS so agents only need to confirm arrival, cutting manual data entry time by up to 40 %. | - Install self‑service kiosks equipped with barcode scanners and a step‑by‑step workflow from NCR SelfServe, allowing guests to complete registration independently while freeing human agents to handle complex cases. | - Conduct a weekly 20‑minute “process sprint” using Miro boards to map bottlenecks, assign owners, and track KPIs (average wait per guest, agent idle time), with a target of ≤10 % variance in wait times month‑over‑month.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant, actionable, and concrete, naming specific tools and measurable outcomes. However, feasibility is scored 2 due to high effort: deploying a cloud queue platform, integrating with POS, launching a mobile app, installing kiosks, and conducting weekly sprints with Miro all require significant investment, cross-team coordination, and time—far beyond trivial or low-effort implementation.

issue: Cabin crew repeatedly bumped into the passenger's seat without apologizing.
fixes: Deploy RFID‑enabled seat sensors linked to a cabin alert dashboard (e.g., via SITA CabinConnect) that triggers an automatic “Seat Collision” notification to the crew’s tablet whenever a crew member approaches a passenger seat, prompting an immediate apology prompt. | - Integrate a mandatory post‑flight micro‑learning module in Cornerstone LMS that requires crew to complete a 2‑minute video scenario on proper aisle navigation and record a brief “apology script”; completion is tracked with a 95 % pass threshold before crews are cleared for duty. | - Install a real‑time passenger feedback kiosk at each galley station (using Medallia Mobile) where passengers can rate “Crew Apology Promptness” on a 1‑5 scale; weekly averages below 4 trigger a corrective action plan reviewed by the service quality team. | - Add a QR‑code‑linked “Apology Log” to each seatback screen that records every crew interaction with a timestamp and logs any missed apologies; the log syncs nightly to the airline’s operations analytics platform for monthly performance KPI reporting.
rationale: While all recommendations are highly relevant, concrete, and actionable in concept, feasibility is rated 1 due to the extreme cost and complexity of deploying RFID-enabled seat sensors, real-time kiosks, and QR-code-linked logs across an entire fleet. These require new hardware, integration with multiple systems (SITA, Medallia, Cornerstone), and operational overhaul—far beyond trivial or low-effort implementation. The proposal ignores budget constraints and assumes unlimited capital, violating the ruthless cost-conscious COO mandate.

review_text: Siem Reap to Kolkata via Singapore. We chose Singapore as we had heard lots of wonderful things about the airline. Our experience was worse than any bucket-shop airlines and we thought Singapore was a world standard airline. On the Siem Reap to Singapore Route my seat had a broken table which I was informed only on the way to the boarding the flight when I was suddenly being pressurised by their ground staff. Although I was waiting at the airport near the gate for over an hour none of your ground staff bothered to discuss this matter with me.  Furthermore the table was only secured with sticky tape which kept falling on my lap and on a couple of occasions fell on my watch! We then received further shoddy service from ground staff at Singapore on the Kolkata flight. Having paid USD 700+ for our one-way ticket from REP to CCU I was shocked to be downgraded to an airline with filthy seats, no TV, no leg space and in general badly in need of an uplift. To cap it all the cabin staff offered me gifts worth SGD 75 on the plane. This offer got progressively downgraded to a few miles on their airline.
issue: Seat had a broken table that was only disclosed at boarding and was poorly secured with sticky tape, causing it to fall on the passenger’s lap and watch
fixes: Deploy a digital “Seat‑Fit Inspection” checklist in the airline’s mobile inspection app (e.g., iAuditor) that crew must complete before every boarding; the checklist requires photo evidence of the table latch and adhesive strength, and logs any deviations for immediate supervisor review. | - Integrate a real‑time seat‑condition dashboard into the cabin crew’s handheld device (e.g., iPad with ServiceNow Field Service Management) that flags any “table‑securement = not met” alerts and auto‑generates a corrective work order for re‑installation within 15 minutes of discovery. | - Install RFID‑enabled seat‑table modules linked to the aircraft’s maintenance management system (e.g., AMOS) so that any module flagged out‑of‑alignment triggers an automatic preventive‑maintenance task before the next flight cycle. | - Publish a standardized SOP video series on the crew’s LMS (e.g., SAP SuccessFactors Learning) that demonstrates proper table latching procedures and adhesive application, with quarterly competency quizzes scored ≥90 % before crew are cleared for duty.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant to the root cause (poorly secured table), explicitly name real tools (iAuditor, ServiceNow, AMOS, SAP SuccessFactors), and are step-by-step actionable. However, feasibility is rated 2 because RFID-enabled modules and real-time dashboard integration require expensive hardware upgrades, software integration, and cross-system coordination—far beyond trivial or low effort. These are not 'existing tools' and would take months and significant budget to implement.

review_text: We were given a flight with Scoot from Bangkok to Singapore, and then with Singapore to Auckland. The flight with Scoot was perfectly acceptable but what was not was that they decided that we must collect our boarding pass in Singapore which might have been OK but they they never told us where to collect them from, so we we spent ages trying to find the location and only just made it to the gate in time. The previous leg from Milan to Hanoi involved a Scoot flight and we received all our passes in Milan. On the flight when meals were served the crew just appeared at the seat with a tray whilst we were dozing and expected us to put out the table. As we had ordered book the cook, the main meal service took a while and we had finished our meal before drinks were offered. Premium economy food was no better than Economy fare and considering the cost of the flight a little more thought could have gone into the meal.
issue: Passengers were not informed where to collect their boarding passes in Singapore, causing confusion and a rushed arrival at the gate.
fixes: Deploy digital signage linked to the airline’s real‑time flight information API (e.g., FlightAware) at all check‑in zones in Singapore, displaying “Boarding Pass Collection – Gate X” with QR codes that passengers can scan for instant directions. | - Integrate the airport’s Wayfinding app (or a custom mobile push notification via Firebase) so that upon ticket issuance, a pop‑up appears on passengers’ phones with step‑by‑step maps and exact location of the designated pass‑collection counter. | - Install self‑service kiosks equipped with NFC readers that automatically print boarding passes and push a SMS/WhatsApp message containing the collection point, ensuring every passenger receives the information before reaching security. | - Add a mandatory pre‑departure checklist item in the crew’s electronic cabin management system (e.g., SITA Cabin) that logs “passenger notified of pass‑collection zone” and triggers a supervisor alert if the task is missed, enabling immediate corrective action.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant, actionable, and concrete, naming specific tools (FlightAware, Firebase, SITA Cabin, NFC, SMS/WhatsApp). However, feasibility is rated 2 due to high effort: deploying digital signage with real-time API integration, integrating with airport Wayfinding apps, installing NFC kiosks, and modifying crew systems require significant capital, cross-team coordination, and time—far beyond trivial or low-effort implementation.

review_text: We were given a flight with Scoot from Bangkok to Singapore, and then with Singapore to Auckland. The flight with Scoot was perfectly acceptable but what was not was that they decided that we must collect our boarding pass in Singapore which might have been OK but they they never told us where to collect them from, so we we spent ages trying to find the location and only just made it to the gate in time. The previous leg from Milan to Hanoi involved a Scoot flight and we received all our passes in Milan. On the flight when meals were served the crew just appeared at the seat with a tray whilst we were dozing and expected us to put out the table. As we had ordered book the cook, the main meal service took a while and we had finished our meal before drinks were offered. Premium economy food was no better than Economy fare and considering the cost of the flight a little more thought could have gone into the meal.
issue: In-flight crew served meals while passengers were dozing and expected tables to be set, leading to poor service timing and inconvenience.
fixes: Deploy a digital “Meal Service Timer” integrated with the aircraft’s cabin management system (e.g., Lufthansa Systems’ Vantage) that automatically alerts crew when passenger sleep‑status sensors (via seat‑back sensors) indicate ≥80% of seats are in recline mode, prompting a 10‑minute delay before tray delivery. | - Introduce a standardized “Pre‑Dining Checklist” on tablet tablets (e.g., SITA CabinConnect) that crew must complete within 5 minutes of reaching their assigned galley, including setting table linens, cutlery, and beverage stations; completion is logged and audited daily via the airline’s Ops Dashboard. | - Schedule a weekly 15‑minute “Timing Review Huddle” using a shared Google Sheet where crew record actual meal‑service start times against planned windows; adjust catering load times by ±2 minutes based on the sheet’s variance report to align with passenger wake cycles. | - Implement QR‑code feedback loops at each row that trigger a real‑time alert to the service manager’s Slack channel when a passenger indicates they were disturbed during meal service, enabling immediate corrective action and tracking disturbance incidents per flight.
rationale: Relevance and concreteness are perfect—each recommendation targets the core issue of poor timing and disturbance during in-flight service with specific tools (Vantage, SITA CabinConnect, Slack, Google Sheets). Actionability is strong, though minor interpretation is needed (e.g., ‘adjust catering load times’). Feasibility is severely limited: sleep sensors, real-time QR feedback, and automated alerts require new hardware, software integration, and cross-system coordination—high effort, not trivial. Scored 2 due to high cost and implementation complexity, violating the 'trivial to implement' threshold for a 5.

review_text: The ground crew were unreasonable and very rude. We were denied boarding as according to them we were 4 minutes late.  The ground crew did a lousy job of offloading our luggage. We had to wait almost half a day in the baggage carousel before filling a missing bag report.
issue: Ground crew were rude and denied boarding for a minor 4‑minute delay
fixes: Deploy a real‑time passenger‑service monitoring dashboard (e.g., Tableau connected to the airline’s OpsCenter) that flags any ground‑crew interaction rated ≤ 4/10 by passengers within 15 minutes; trigger an automatic alert to the floor manager for immediate coaching. | - Introduce a “Delay Compensation Workflow” in ServiceNow that automatically issues a $25 voucher via email within 24 hours whenever a boarding denial is logged, ensuring consistent resolution and reducing friction. | - Launch weekly 20‑minute VR scenario simulations on Oculus Quest where staff rehearse handling minor delays, with performance tracked through the LMS and tied to a quarterly KPI target of ≥ 90 % positive post‑interaction surveys. | - Implement a power‑BI Power Query script that cross‑references crew shift logs with delay timestamps, flagging any crew member who denies boarding more than twice per month for mandatory remedial training. | - Create a mobile “Passenger Voice” app feature that allows passengers to rate crew interactions instantly, feeding data into a daily pulse report reviewed by operations leadership to identify recurring behavior patterns.
rationale: Relevance is 5: All recommendations directly address the root issue of rude crew behavior and boarding denial due to minor delays. Concreteness is 5: Each recommendation specifies real tools (Tableau, ServiceNow, Oculus Quest, Power BI, LMS) and measurable outcomes. Actionability is 4: Steps are clear but require interpretation (e.g., 'trigger an alert'—how? Who receives it?). Feasibility is 2: VR simulations, custom Power BI scripts, and a new mobile app require significant investment, cross-team coordination, and time—far beyond 'low effort' or 'trivial.'

rationale: All recommendations directly address baggage mishandling root causes with specific tools (SITA, ServiceNow, Power BI, Salesforce) and measurable actions. Each is actionable and concrete. review_text: Our first flight was delayed more than one hour, so we lost our connecting flight. The new booking provided was horrible. I was travelling with my four-year-old and the new seat provided is far away, and we were separated. All the family members are separated for the whole journey. Miscommunication between the crew members made us to walk between the terminals for 2 times. Didn't get any help from the crew, even though we requested to provide a seat together.
issue: Flight was delayed over an hour, causing a missed connecting flight
fixes: Deploy a real‑time delay‑management dashboard (e.g., FlightAware Insights) that flags any departure deviation >15 minutes and automatically triggers a “re‑booking” workflow in Sabre’s Red Workspace to offer affected passengers alternative connections within 10 minutes of detection. | - Integrate Twilio SMS/WhatsApp notifications with the airline’s CRM so that passengers receive a personalized message instantly when their scheduled departure slips beyond the defined threshold, including a link to a self‑service re‑booking portal hosted on AWS Amplify. | - Implement a predictive maintenance KPI in the aviation MRO system (e.g., Amossys) targeting a 95 % on‑time dispatch rate; schedule pre‑flight engine run checks based on historical wear‑rate models and log compliance data in Power BI for weekly review. | - Establish a dedicated “Delay Response Team” staffed by two operations analysts per shift who follow a documented SOP in Asana that mandates a maximum 20‑minute response time from receipt of a delay alert to passenger re‑booking or compensation offer.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant to flight delays and rebooking. Each is actionable with clear steps and names specific tools (Sabre, Twilio, Amossys, Power BI, Asana, AWS Amplify). However, feasibility is severely limited: deploying a real-time dashboard with automated workflows across multiple systems (FlightAware, Sabre, Twilio, AWS) requires significant integration, data pipeline development, and cross-team coordination—far beyond 'trivial' or 'low effort'. The predictive maintenance KPI and dedicated team also require major operational restructuring. Thus, feasibility is rated 2 despite strong other scores.

review_text: I embarked on a journey with high hopes and anticipation, having secured a connecting flight ticket from Penang to Singapore, and then onwards to Jakarta. Opting for Singapore Airlines, renowned for its outstanding reputation in both air travel and service, I anticipated nothing less than a seamless experience. The cost of this connecting flight amounted to MYR 1601, roughly USD 335, a price I was more than willing to pay for quality service. My journey commenced with a minor delay in takeoff from Penang. The flight proceeded smoothly, accompanied by a light snack comprising a slice of pizza and a beverage of choice. As we approached Changi Airport, the situation took an unexpected turn. The captain announced a delay in landing, estimating an additional 15 to 20 minutes before touchdown. A sense of unease crept over me as my connecting flight departure time loomed closer. Despite my concerns, I remained optimistic, trusting in the efficiency of Singapore Airlines to navigate any challenges seamlessly. Upon landing at 12:05 pm and disembarking at 12:18 pm, I was met with a startling realization. Rather than a smooth transition to my connecting flight, I was handed a new ticket, rescheduled for departure at 4:10 pm, accompanied by a SGD 15 food voucher for Burger King and Coffee Bean.  I sought clarity from the attendant, only to be met with dismissiveness. The absence of any acknowledgment or attempt at redress left me deeply disappointed. The disregard for my time and the indifferent handling of the situation underscored a gap in the airline's commitment to customer service. In retrospect, my expectation for exemplary hospitality and proactive problem-solving from a reputed airline like Singapore Airlines was regrettably unmet. As I reflect on this experience, I am reminded of the importance of genuine care and consideration in customer service, particularly in moments of inconvenience or distress.
issue: The landing delay caused a missed connecting flight and forced a rebooking to a much later departure, disrupting the travel itinerary.
fixes: Deploy a real‑time disruption dashboard in Amadeus Altéa (or Sabre AirVision) that automatically flags any landing delay > 15 minutes and triggers an immediate “hold‑the‑connection” alert to the ground operations team via Slack. | - Integrate Twilio SMS/WhatsApp notifications into the airline’s mobile app so passengers receive a personalized message with alternative gate information and a one‑click rebooking link within 5 minutes of the delay. | - Implement a predictive analytics model in Azure Machine Learning that uses historical weather and aircraft performance data to generate pre‑emptive buffer times for high‑traffic routes; adjust schedule margins accordingly before the flight is published. | - Launch a dedicated “Disruption Recovery” queue in Zendesk with a 20‑minute SLA, staffed by cross‑trained agents who use the same rebooking engine as the original booking to offer passengers at least two viable rebooking options within the hour.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant to flight delay disruption, crystal clear in actionability, and explicitly name real tools (Amadeus Altéa, Twilio, Azure ML, Zendesk). However, feasibility is rated 2: implementing predictive analytics in Azure ML, integrating Twilio at scale, and deploying a real-time dashboard in legacy systems like Amadeus Altéa require significant engineering effort, cross-team coordination, and budget—far beyond trivial or low effort. These are not 'today' fixes, violating strict feasibility.

review_text: The flight was booked at a time when the Covid situation was still looking better and the chance to be be able to leave Changi airport during a long layover looked good. That face with Singapore Airlines flexibility claim of change of flights on their website, made me booking a flight with a 16h layover. Unfortunately, things were not looking good about a month ago, and I tried to change my flights to a short layover. I tried to contact SQ via its change of flight form for Covid related reasons through their website. 5 requests over the span of a month remain unanswered till today, complaint emails were also not replied to. I also tried to contact SQ through their callcenter, was placed in a loop for hours. After getting through after days of trying, I was told that there were seats available for the same booking class and I would be informed once the the ticket would be re-issued. So far so good. An hour later I was called, saying that this „special fare ticket“ can only be changed if if I pay this and that in addition, which ended up to a ridiculous amount, and I declined. So I was back to square and had to endure a 16h stay at Changi airport. SQ does not honor their claim of ticket flexibility. Other carrier I flew during the pandemic were willing honor their flexibility claims. The flights themselves were rather unremarkable, not as polished as Qatar, with no dining on demand offered. Food was average to say the least. No touchscreen TV and average seat on the really noisy 777ER on the long flight. Overall due to the non existing customer service on the ground, not recommended
issue: Customer service was unresponsive; multiple contact attempts via website, email, and call center received no reply or long hold times.
fixes: Deploy a ticketing system (e.g., Zendesk) integrated with the website’s “Contact Us” form and auto‑reply email trigger so every inquiry creates a trackable ticket instantly and generates an acknowledgment within 2 minutes. | - Implement a real‑time queue dashboard in Talkdesk that displays live wait‑times and agent availability, setting an SLA of ≤ 2 minutes average answer time and automatically routing calls to a backup queue when thresholds are breached. | - Create a dedicated “Rapid Response Team” with a rotating shift schedule managed through WhenIWork, assigning two agents per shift to handle escalated tickets flagged by the ticketing system within 15 minutes of creation. | - Send automated follow‑up emails via Mailchimp at 24‑hour intervals for any ticket remaining unresolved, including a clickable “Need help now?” button that routes the case back to the same agent’s queue for immediate resolution.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the customer’s core issue of unresponsiveness and long wait times. Each is highly actionable, specifies real tools (Zendesk, Talkdesk, WhenIWork, Mailchimp), and includes measurable actions. Feasibility is 3 because while tools are named, implementing a new Rapid Response Team and routing logic requires cross-team coordination, training, and integration effort beyond trivial setup, making it moderate rather than low effort.

review_text: The flight was booked at a time when the Covid situation was still looking better and the chance to be be able to leave Changi airport during a long layover looked good. That face with Singapore Airlines flexibility claim of change of flights on their website, made me booking a flight with a 16h layover. Unfortunately, things were not looking good about a month ago, and I tried to change my flights to a short layover. I tried to contact SQ via its change of flight form for Covid related reasons through their website. 5 requests over the span of a month remain unanswered till today, complaint emails were also not replied to. I also tried to contact SQ through their callcenter, was placed in a loop for hours. After getting through after days of trying, I was told that there were seats available for the same booking class and I would be informed once the the ticket would be re-issued. So far so good. An hour later I was called, saying that this „special fare ticket“ can only be changed if if I pay this and that in addition, which ended up to a ridiculous amount, and I declined. So I was back to square and had to endure a 16h stay at Changi airport. SQ does not honor their claim of ticket flexibility. Other carrier I flew during the pandemic were willing honor their flexibility claims. The flights themselves were rather unremarkable, not as polished as Qatar, with no dining on demand offered. Food was average to say the least. No touchscreen TV and average seat on the really noisy 777ER on the long flight. Overall due to the non existing customer service on the ground, not recommended
issue: Lack of dining on demand and average-quality food during the flight.
fixes: Deploy a cloud‑based catering management platform (e.g., CaterTrax) integrated with the airline’s reservation system so passengers can pre‑order meals up to 24 hours before departure and receive real‑time status updates via the airline’s mobile app. | - Install IoT temperature monitoring sensors in all galley refrigerators and cargo holds; configure them to trigger alerts when temperatures deviate >±2 °C from set points, ensuring food stays within optimal freshness windows. | - Introduce a “Fresh‑Cooked” menu option by partnering with local restaurants and using a kitchen‑management dashboard (e.g., Kitchen United) to schedule weekly delivery of fresh ingredients and track preparation times per flight. | - Launch a post‑flight micro‑survey in the airline’s CRM (Salesforce Service Cloud) asking passengers to rate meal quality on a 1‑5 scale; automatically route scores ≤ 3 to a dedicated catering QA team for immediate corrective action.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant to food quality and dining-on-demand issues. Each is actionable with clear steps and names specific tools (CaterTrax, Kitchen United, Salesforce Service Cloud). Concreteness is maximal with named systems and measurable thresholds. However, feasibility is low: IoT sensor deployment in cargo holds, integrating with reservation systems, and partnering with local restaurants for weekly deliveries require significant capital, logistics, and cross-functional coordination—far beyond trivial or low effort. Thus, feasibility scores 2 despite strong other dimensions.

fixes: Deploy cabin‑wide noise‑canceling headset kits (e.g., Bose QuietComfort™ 35 II) on all seats and integrate them with the airline’s seat‑back entertainment system via the existing USB‑C port, completing installation within 90 days. | - Replace standard economy seats with ergonomic “Premium Economy” recliners sourced from Recaro Aircraft Seating and install them on at least 20% of aircraft by Q3 2024, tracking completion in the maintenance management system (e.g., AMOS). | - Implement a real‑time sound‑level monitoring dashboard powered by Brüel & Kjær SoundLevel sensors linked to the flight operations center; set a daily alert threshold of 70 dB(A) and trigger immediate crew action when exceeded. | - Launch a post‑flight comfort survey using Qualtrics with a mandatory “Noise Rating” question, aiming for a ≥10% reduction in negative feedback scores within six months and review results weekly in the passenger insights team meeting.

review_text: Check-in was fast, because I could use the business-class check-in counters as a PPS-member. Otherwise endless queues. Boarding was slow because the flight was full and I boarded late with the crowds. No recognition as a Solitaire-PPS-member on board as before. Cabin crew busy with 'sorting' the passengers and their cabin-luggage to not miss our slot. Seat in a 3-3-3 configuration very narrow, seat-pitch ok (for me being 1.7m only) sufficient, however. Left channel of my speakers was not working, even after exchanging them. Internet not working on my phone, either. No help given to connect, as on earlier flights, instead 'proof' was given to me by a flight attendant that Internet was working on her iphone. Not really helpful. No reading materials. Very basic and little food-options offered in a tiny cardboard-box, 'designed for a reason' (whatever this reason may be). Very inattentive inflight-cabin-crew: As for the after dinner drink I was ignored by the them while my two seat-members got served their drinks of choice. Minimum seat-recline, similar to those in budget-airlines. No soap in washroom. No disinfectant-kits offered as advertised, I got one after request only. Wearing a mask, which is 'mandatory' was not re-enforced, a man in the middle seat was wearing his mask under his chin - no one of the crew cared. Overall the crew seemed to be overworked and de-motivated. The typical glass of champagne offered to me as a PPS-member when I raised my concerns did not really help, it just would have been good enough if the cabin-crew would have fulfilled their duties. Disembarking was fast and swift as well as delivery of my checked-in baggage.
issue: Boarding was slow due to a full flight and crew having to sort passengers and luggage
fixes: Deploy RFID‑enabled boarding passes linked to a real‑time queue‑management dashboard (e.g., QLess) so crew see passenger arrival times on tablets and automatically call groups when space becomes available. | - Integrate the airline’s reservation system with an automated baggage‑sorting workflow in the Amadeus Altéa Baggage Handling module, routing excess bags to off‑site storage before gate closure. | - Use a predictive‑analytics model built in Azure Machine Learning to forecast peak boarding windows 15 minutes ahead and trigger pre‑boarding announcements via airport PA systems and mobile push alerts. | - Implement a “fast‑lane” boarding protocol using a handheld barcode scanner (e.g., Zebra DS3600) at the door that scans a QR code printed on priority tickets, cutting average boarding time by up to 40 seconds per passenger.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant to boarding delays, specify exact tools (e.g., QLess, Amadeus Altéa, Zebra DS3600), and include measurable actions. They are actionable and concrete. However, feasibility is rated 2 because deploying RFID-enabled passes, integrating Amadeus Altéa with off-site baggage storage, and building a predictive model in Azure Machine Learning require significant capital, system integration, and coordination—far beyond trivial or low-effort changes. These are not 'today' solutions for a budget-conscious COO.

issue: Loyalty status (Solitaire-PPS) was not recognized on board
fixes: Deploy a real‑time PPS integration with the airline’s reservation system (e.g., Amadeus Altéa) via API calls so that Solitaire status is automatically pulled from the loyalty database and displayed on boarding passes at check‑in. | - Configure the crew’s mobile service app (e.g., SITA CabinConnect) to include a “Loyalty Status” tile that pulls the latest tier information from the central PPS server, ensuring cabin staff see current status during pre‑flight briefing. | - Set up an automated validation rule in Salesforce Service Cloud that flags any ticket where the passenger’s Solitaire tier is missing; assign a 24‑hour SLA to re‑sync the profile before the next flight. | - Add a QR‑code scan checkpoint at the gate kiosk that cross‑checks the printed boarding pass against the live Solitaire record, prompting an instant update if the status is out of sync. | - Schedule a weekly reconciliation script in Azure Functions that compares all active bookings against the PPS master table, logging mismatches and triggering corrective updates to the booking engine.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant, actionable, and concrete, naming specific tools (Amadeus Altéa, SITA CabinConnect, Salesforce Service Cloud, Azure Functions) and measurable actions. However, feasibility is rated 2 because deploying real-time API integrations, configuring mobile apps, setting up automated validation rules, and implementing QR-code gate kiosks require significant cross-system integration, vendor coordination, and infrastructure changes—far beyond trivial or low-effort implementation. These are high-effort, high-cost changes that cannot be rolled out 'today' with existing tools.

The typical glass of champagne offered to me as a PPS-member when I raised my concerns did not really help, it just would have been good enough if the cabin-crew would have fulfilled their duties. Disembarking was fast and swift as well as delivery of my checked-in baggage.
issue: Cabin crew ignored the passenger’s after‑dinner drink request, showing inattentiveness
fixes: Deploy a cabin‑service mobile app (e.g., ServiceNow Mobile) that automatically logs every passenger beverage request with timestamp; set an alert if a request remains unfulfilled for more than 10 minutes. | - Introduce a “Request‑Acknowledgment” button on seat‑back screens linked to the crew’s handheld device (iPad + AirWatch); crew must tap the button within 2 minutes of receiving a request, creating a real‑time audit trail. | - Schedule weekly 15‑minute “Micro‑Audit” meetings where the lead flight attendant reviews the logged requests from the past week, flags any missed drinks, and assigns corrective actions tracked in a shared Google Sheet. | - Launch a quarterly 45‑minute VR scenario‑based training module on the Strivr platform focused on “after‑meal service timing,” requiring ≥90 % pass rate before crew can fly the next cycle. | - Tie a KPI to the ServiceNow data – “% of after‑dinner drink requests fulfilled within 5 minutes” – and publish it on the crew dashboard, linking fulfillment rates to performance bonuses.
rationale: Relevance is 5: all recommendations directly address inattentiveness to after-dinner drink requests. Actionability is 4: steps are clear but assume existing infrastructure (e.g., AirWatch, Strivr) without confirming availability. Concreteness is 5: specific tools (ServiceNow, Strivr, AirWatch), metrics (% fulfilled in 5 min), and exact timeframes are named. Feasibility is 2: VR training and system-wide app deployment require major investment, cross-team coordination, and months of rollout—far from trivial or low effort.

issue: No soap was provided in the washroom
fixes: Deploy a cloud‑based inventory management system (e.g., **Zoho Inventory**) with automatic reorder alerts set to trigger when soap stock falls below a 7‑day buffer, ensuring real‑time replenishment. | - Install RFID‑enabled soap dispensers linked to the building’s IoT dashboard so any dispenser drop‑below‑threshold is flagged instantly and staff receive a push notification via **Microsoft Teams** for immediate refill. | - Create a weekly “Washroom Hygiene Checklist” in **Google Forms**, assigning it to the facilities team; if any item (soap availability) remains unchecked after 24 hours, an automated email reminder is sent through **Outlook Flow**. | - Contract a local vendor to deliver pre‑packed soap kits on a fixed schedule using a **Kanban board** in **Jira**, with a KPI of 95 % on‑time delivery tracked and reviewed monthly.
rationale: All recommendations are relevant, actionable, and concrete, naming specific tools and measurable actions. However, feasibility is low: deploying RFID dispensers, integrating IoT dashboards, and managing Kanban in Jira with a third-party vendor for soap kits requires significant capital, cross-team coordination, and operational overhaul—far beyond trivial or low effort. This makes the proposal high-effort and impractical for immediate deployment.

fixes: Deploy QR‑code signage at entry points linked to a “Request Disinfectant Kit” button in the mobile app (e.g., via Square Online) so guests can instantly trigger delivery of a kit without staff involvement. | - Add a mandatory “Offer Disinfectant Kit” step to the POS checkout workflow in Toast POS, with automatic logging of completion and non‑completion rates for weekly review. | - Integrate iAuditor’s digital checklist into daily floor audits, requiring supervisors to confirm that disinfectant kits are placed on tables before opening and documenting any omissions. | - Set up a real‑time KPI dashboard in Power BI that pulls data from the POS “Kit Offered” flag; if >10 % of transactions lack a kit offer, schedule a corrective‑action meeting within 24 hours.

Not trivial (5), but achievable within weeks with existing tools.

fixes: Deploy a real‑time mask compliance dashboard in the airline’s operations center powered by Azure IoT Hub sensors on seat‑back screens; any passenger flagging improper mask use triggers an automatic alert to the nearest crew member within 15 seconds. | - Integrate a “Mask Check” button into the crew’s mobile app (e.g., CrewConnect) that logs each inspection and automatically records a photo of the passenger’s face cover; compliance rates are tracked weekly via a Power BI report with a target ≥98 % enforcement. | - Conduct random 10‑minute spot checks during boarding and gate changes using handheld thermal cameras linked to the crew’s tablet app, generating instant violation reports that feed into the daily safety audit log. | - Implement a quarterly KPI audit in Qualtrics where passengers rate mask enforcement on a 5‑point scale; tie crew bonuses to achieving ≥90 % positive ratings and publish results on the intranet leaderboard.

issue: Crew appeared overworked and demotivated, affecting overall service
fixes: Deploy a workforce‑management platform (e.g., Kronos WFM) integrated with real‑time shift‑demand analytics to generate optimal staffing schedules that keep average crew load below 90 % of capacity, measured weekly via the built‑in KPI dashboard. | - Launch a gamified incentive program in the existing HRIS (e.g., BambooHR) that awards points for on‑time arrivals, customer satisfaction scores ≥ 85 %, and attendance compliance, redeemable for quarterly bonus credits tracked automatically through the system’s reporting module. | - Institute a daily 15‑minute stand‑up huddle where supervisors review the “Employee Engagement Pulse” survey results from SurveyMonkey (target score ≥ 80/100) and assign one tangible task per crew member to boost morale, documented in a shared Google Sheet for accountability. | - Contract a third‑party auditing firm to conduct quarterly mystery‑shopper visits focusing on service quality and crew demeanor; feed findings into a corrective‑action workflow in Asana with owner assignments and due dates, ensuring remedial actions are completed within two weeks.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant, actionable, and concrete, naming specific tools and metrics. These are not 'today' fixes and violate the strict feasibility threshold for a 4 or 5.
